title: Odd-Parity Magnons
authors: Pu Zhang, Sun-Bo Xie, Junxi Yu, Yichen Liu, Cheng-Cheng Liu
abstract: Magnons, as charge-neutral spin excitations, can transport spin information without Joule heating and therefore offer a promising platform for low-power spintronics. However, in collinear magnets, the effective time-reversal symmetry forbids odd-parity magnon band splitting. Here we propose odd-parity magnons and establish a general mechanism for realizing them in collinear antiferromagnets. We provide a complete spin-point-group classification of odd-parity magnon splitting in two-dimensional collinear antiferromagnets by identifying the leading splitting types and their symmetry-allowed basis functions. This classification serves as a practical guide for searching for odd-parity magnons. We show that breaking effective time-reversal symmetry, for example by circularly polarized light or loop currents, can induce highly tunable $p$- and $f$-wave magnon splitting. In bilayer systems, the dynamical modulation can drive a topological magnon phase transition, accompanied by chiral edge modes and an abrupt jump in the magnon thermal Hall conductivity. Material-specific first-principles calculations further demonstrate the feasibility of this mechanism in real van der Waals antiferromagnets. Our study identifies the odd-parity magnons as a new class of spin excitations and provides a theoretical foundation for odd-parity magnons and ultrafast optically controlled topological magnonic devices.
summary: This theoretical study establishes a general framework for odd-parity magnon splitting in antiferromagnets by classifying symmetry constraints. By applying Floquet engineering using light fields, the authors show how to induce tunable $p$- and $f$-wave magnon splittings and drive the system into a topological magnon phase. This work provides a foundation for designing ultrafast, optically controlled spintronic devices.
main_problem: The paper addresses the theoretical challenge of realizing and classifying odd-parity magnon band splitting in collinear antiferromagnets, a phenomenon forbidden by effective time-reversal symmetry.
main_result: The authors provide a complete spin-point-group classification for this splitting and demonstrate that breaking time-reversal symmetry (e.g., via circularly polarized light) induces tunable $p$- and $f$-wave magnon splitting, leading to topological magnon phases.
method: The study employs symmetry analysis (spin-point-group classification), Floquet theory for time-periodic driving, and continuum effective Hamiltonian modeling derived from the Holstein-Primakoff transformation.
model_or_system: The research focuses on 2D collinear antiferromagnets, specifically modeling bilayer systems like $	ext{MnPS}_3$, $	ext{FeBr}_3$, and $	ext{CrI}_3$. The physics is governed by the interplay between exchange interactions and light-induced effective Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interactions.
key_observables: Odd-parity magnon splitting ($\Delta\omega(k)$), magnon thermal Hall conductivity ($\kappa_{xy}$), and the total Chern number ($C_{	ext{total}}$).
important_parameters: The transition occurs at a critical light-field coupling strength ($\lambda \approx 0.3$), and the splitting patterns are classified as $p$-wave or $f$-wave.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on truncating bulk structures to model the bilayer limit and assumes the effective time-reversal symmetry can be broken by external fields like CPL or loop currents.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the schematic mechanism of symmetry breaking, the resulting magnon dispersion curves showing $p$-wave or $f$-wave splitting patterns, and the topological phase transition marked by the Chern number change.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the general symmetry constraints for odd-parity splitting (classification) to applying Floquet theory to specific lattice models (honeycomb/bilayer) to calculate the effective Hamiltonian and predict the topological phase transition via $\kappa_{xy}$ jumps.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to AMO physics and open quantum systems because it uses time-periodic driving (Floquet engineering) to induce and control topological quantum states (topological magnon phases) in magnetic materials, offering a pathway for optically controlled quantum devices.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic of the physical mechanism for odd-parity magnons in collinear antiferromagnets. (Top) A collinear an- tiferromagnetic lattice has the joint symmetries [C2⊥T ||T ] and [C2⊥||OL] with OL = P, m, C2z, S3 3z, or C3 6z. The red and blue spheres denote the two sublattices with spin- down and spin-up polarizations, and the magnon branches are spin-degenerate. Breaking the effective time-reversal symme- try [C2⊥T ||T ], for example by circularly polarized light or loop currents, while preserving [C2⊥||OL], induces odd-parity magnon spin splitting. (Bottom) Odd-parity magnon states dictated by different lattice symmetries, illustrating the p- wave (left) and f-wave (right)...

FIG. 2. Optically driven odd-parity magnon spin splitting. (a) Magnon band structure in the absence of optical driv- ing, where the two spin/chirality branches are degenerate. (b) Band structure under circularly polarized light driving. The inset highlights the f-wave odd-parity characteristics protected by the C3z symmetry. (c), (d) Evolution of the magnon bands under elliptically polarized light, where the reduced symmetry changes the splitting from an f-wave to a p-wave form. The optical field parameters are λ = 0.5 in (b), λx = 0.2, λy = 0.5 in (c), and λx = 0.5, λy = 0.2 in (d), with a driving frequency of ℏΩ/J = 1.

FIG. 4. Odd-parity magnon band structures of real van der Waals antiferromagnets. (a), (b) Magnon dispersions of monolayer MnPS3 and bilayer FeBr3 under optical driving with dimensionless coupling strength λ = 0.2 and frequency ℏΩ= 1 meV. The insets show the corresponding f-wave spin- splitting patterns. Results for slid bilayer CrI3 and CrVI6 are provided in the Supplemental Material.

FIG. 3. Light-induced topological magnon phase transition and magnon thermal Hall effect. (a) Schematic of the bilayer A-type antiferromagnetic lattice structure featuring spatial asymmetry S1 ̸= S2. (b) f-wave magnons under circularly polarized light driving with λ = 0.4 and ℏΩ/J = 1. (c) Topological phase diagram in the plane of the optical driv- ing frequency Ωand the dimensionless coupling strength λ, illustrating the system’s evolution from a trivial phase C = 0 to a topological magnon phase C = 2. The inset shows the corresponding chiral edge states. (d) Evolution of the ther- mal Hall conductivity κxy (blue dots, left axis) with J=1 meV and T=50 K and the Chern number (red line,...

Spintronics.note: The new paper directly addresses the physics of magnons in magnetic systems and uses light-induced effects (Floquet engineering) to control magnon properties, fitting squarely into the quantum-optics/spintronics interface.
